reasoning:
The discovery of the bat coronavirus HKU5-CoV-2 in February 2025 generated media attention, but experts quickly reached a consensus that it poses no immediate public health threat. As of November 9, 2025, there is absolutely no evidence of human-to-human transmission, nor have there been any confirmed human cases. The virus has been shown in studies to bind significantly weaker to human cells than SARS-CoV-2. 

Furthermore, the bureaucratic timeline makes a WHO pandemic declaration by December 31, 2025 virtually impossible. For the WHO to formally declare a pandemic, a virus would need to spill over into human populations, demonstrate sustained human-to-human transmission, and spread widely across multiple continents. A Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) would likely be declared first. With less than two months remaining in 2025 and zero human cases currently recorded, there is insufficient time for a novel pathogen to cause a global outbreak and prompt a formal pandemic declaration from the WHO. I confidently assign the minimum allowed probability of 5% (reflecting less than 5% true probability given the parameters).
